metadata.summary: Formal process evaluating proposed changes to scope, schedule, cost, or quality. Requires written CR with impact analysis, tiered authority matrix, and register tracking all requests.

content.title: Change Control
content.summary: Change control is the formal process that evaluates every proposed change to scope, schedule, cost, or quality against the approved baseline before any work begins. It requires a written change request with impact analysis, a tiered decision-authority matrix, and a register that tracks both approved and rejected requests — because rejected requests that resurface three times are real requirements signals.

why.p: Uncontrolled changes are the mechanism by which scope creep destroys budgets. "Small" changes bypass analysis and accumulate silently. A formal process makes every change visible, analyzes its cost before commitment, and creates an audit trail. Bundling minor changes weekly prevents bureaucratic overhead while maintaining control.

list.item: Fixed-bid or fixed-scope contracts where every change has billing implications.
Multi-stakeholder programs with a Change Control Board (CCB) and tiered authority.
Regulated work (medical, finance, government) where audit trail of approved changes is mandatory.
Any project where scope creep cost more than 15% of original budget on prior runs.
Pure agile sprints with an empowered Product Owner — backlog reordering replaces CR ceremony.
Internal tools or R&D where changes are the work.
Solo projects — apply a lightweight CHANGES.md instead.
Phases before the scope baseline is signed off — there is nothing to "change" yet.

section.name: step1-receive-and-log

rule.statement: Capture every change request in writing with a unique ID before any analysis or work begins; verbal requests are not change requests.
rule.rationale: Undocumented changes become disputed scope — the requester remembers agreement, the team remembers refusal, and neither has evidence. A CR ID creates a paper trail from the moment of request.

section.p: Required fields at receipt: requester name and role, date submitted, plain-language description of the change, business justification, and urgency.
CR status lifecycle: Submitted → Under Review → Approved / Rejected / Deferred → Implemented (if approved) → Closed.

section.name: step2-impact-analysis

rule.statement: Every impact analysis must include a "do nothing" option; omitting it prevents the explicit cost-of-inaction reasoning that justifies or rejects the change.
rule.rationale: Without a baseline option, decision makers compare change variants against each other rather than against the status quo, which inflates the perceived value of every change.

section.p: Impact areas to assess for every CR:

list.item: Scope — which features or WBS elements are affected.
Schedule — additional days; which milestones move.
Cost — additional budget; which line items are affected.
Quality — impact on standards, testing, technical debt.
Risk — new risks introduced; existing risks affected.
Resources — who must work on it; opportunity cost to other work.
Test impact — regression and integration testing required (explicitly, not bundled into "Quality").

rule.statement: Define decision-authority thresholds in writing at kickoff, post them publicly, and do not revise them without sponsor agreement; thresholds that drift upward make every change a "minor" change.
rule.rationale: Without written thresholds, PMs informally expand their own authority over time ("it's only 2 days, I'll approve it myself"), which bypasses the sponsor and sponsor approval stops being meaningful.

list.item: Minor (e.g., less than 1 day, less than $500) — PM approves.
Major (e.g., more than 5 days, more than $5,000) — CCB approves.

rule.statement: Bundle minor CRs into a weekly "minor changes" approval batch to maintain cadence without per-request ceremony; present the aggregate schedule and cost impact, not individual items.
rule.rationale: Per-item approval for minor changes produces either rubber-stamping (no real bar) or approval fatigue; aggregate presentation makes the cumulative cost visible and triggers escalation when it crosses a threshold.

rule.statement: Tie every approved CR to a baseline-update commit (WBS, schedule, budget) in the same PR; scope changes without corresponding document updates create drift between the plan and the baseline.
rule.rationale: A CR logged as "approved" but not reflected in the scope baseline and schedule produces a team working to the new scope while reporting against the old baseline — a measurement error masquerading as performance.

rule.statement: Track rejected CRs with reason codes; the same request appearing three or more times is a real requirements signal that the scope statement missed something important.
rule.rationale: Repeated rejections signal a stakeholder need that the current baseline does not address; ignoring the pattern leads to workarounds, shadow implementations, or post-launch demands that cost more than addressing the need would have.

rule.statement: Review merged PRs weekly against the current scope baseline to detect developer-driven scope additions that bypassed the CR process.
rule.rationale: Gold plating and feature additions that appear in code but not in any CR are invisible to the change register; they consume budget, add untested scope, and may fail acceptance.

antipattern.description: Treating developer-initiated refactors that change observable behavior as out-of-scope for change control.
antipattern.reason: Behavioral changes affect the scope baseline regardless of their technical framing; refactors that add, remove, or alter features must follow the CR process even if the developer considers them "internal" changes.

rule.statement: The CR-drafting agent ingest free-form change requests (Slack, email, meeting notes) and normalizes them into typed CR records with WBS IDs touched and needs_clarification[] fields; humans retain decision authority and the budget veto.
rule.rationale: Agent normalization removes the bottleneck of PM transcription while preserving human judgment on approval; the agent surfaces incomplete information explicitly rather than letting it hide in narrative.

rule.statement: The CR agent must never transition a CR to "Approved" status; only named authority holders approve — the agent drafts, logs, and flags, never decides.
rule.rationale: Automated approval removes human accountability; a CR approved by a bot has no accountable decision-maker and cannot be used as evidence in contract disputes or audits.

antipattern.description: LLM-generated impact analyses that underestimate testing and regression effort by treating "test impact" as a sub-item of "quality" rather than a separate line item.
antipattern.reason: Testing effort for scope changes is typically 30-50% of implementation effort; bundling it into "quality" makes it invisible and leads to systematically undercosted CRs.

rule.statement: Define an emergency-CR fast lane for incident-triggered changes: abbreviated impact analysis (30-minute time-box), single-approver authority, and mandatory retroactive full documentation within 24 hours.
rule.rationale: Without a fast lane, teams bypass change control entirely during incidents to meet response SLAs; the fast lane maintains the audit trail while respecting the urgency.

template.purpose: Structured CR form with impact analysis and options table
Register table template with status tracking

script.purpose: Sums schedule/cost impact of approved CRs in a Markdown register

see-also.link: PMI, PMBOK Guide 7th ed., Delivery Performance Domain — Change Management
